text: The surgeon isolates the superior mesenteric vein, followed by clearing the area around the superior mesenteric artery (SMA). The pancreatic head and duodenum are retracted anteriorly and to the left to facilitate proximal SMA dissection.
The surgeon performs posterior dissection of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) using electrocautery, carefully removing the perineural sheath from the vessel to optimize oncologic margins.
The surgeon dissects, ligates, and transects the pancreaticoduodenal artery, followed by completing the periportal lymph node dissection, mobilizing the surrounding tissue with the specimen.

The specimen is retracted inferiorly as the surgeon begins isolating the portal vein. A vessel loop is placed around the portal vein, and a graft for venous reconstruction is created from the left renal vein.

The surgeon divides the insertion of the vessel into the inferior vena cava (IVC) using a stapler. The distal stump is sharply transected and over-sewn with running VivoProLean suture. The left gonadal vein is preserved to ensure venous drainage of the kidney.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein, ligates it, and transects it. Subsequently, the splenic vein is also ligated and transected.
The surgeon performs the final dissection of the anterior attachments to the superior mesenteric artery, leaving the specimen attached solely by the portal vein and superior mesenteric vein.
The left gastric vein is visibly preserved, ensuring venous drainage of the stomach.
The surgeon places a clamp on the superior mesenteric vein, followed by a clamp on the portal vein. The portal vein is then transected, completing the isolation process.

The surgeon completes the separation of the specimen from its final attachment to the superior mesenteric vein. The specimen is placed in the left upper quadrant, and reconstruction begins immediately to minimize clamp time.

The surgeon positions the renal vein graft and begins the proximal anastomosis using a running 5-0 Prolene suture. The superior mesenteric artery is clamped to minimize edema during the reconstruction. The distal anastomosis between the portal vein and the renal graft is completed with a running 5-0 Prolene suture.
The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to verify the patency and flow of the renal vein graft.
The surgeon removes the clamps in sequence, starting with the distal clamp, followed by the proximal clamp, and finally the superior mesenteric artery clamp, after completing the anastomosis.
